result.markdown:
--- title: Comparison with pandas author: Marie-Hélène Burle bibliography: python.bib csl: ../diabetologia.csl --- :::{.def} As pandas was the only DataFrame library for Python for a long time, many Python users are familiar with it and a comparison with Polars might be useful. ::: ## Overview | | pandas | Polars | |--|--|--| | Available for | Python | Rust, Python, R, NodeJS | | Written in | Cython | Rust | | Multithreading | Some operations | Yes (GIL released) | | Index | Rows are indexed | Integer positions are used | | Evaluation | Eager | Eager and lazy | | Query optimizer | No | Yes | | Out-of-core | No | Yes | | [SIMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_data) vectorization | Yes | Yes | | Data in memory | With [NumPy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) arrays | With [Apache Arrow](https://github.com/apache/arrow) arrays | | Memory efficiency | Poor | Excellent | | Handling of missing data | Inconsistent | Consistent, promotes type stability | ## Missing data ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="52%"} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="25%"} For historical reasons, depending on dtype, missing values are one of: - `NaN` (`numpy.nan`, dtype float), - `None`, - `NaT` (Not-a-Time, dtype datetime), - `pandas.NA` (nullable scalar). This leads to inconsistent behaviour and unexpected dtype changes. There has been an [ongoing open issue since 2020](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/32265). ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="46%"}
![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="30%"} One single missing value: `null`. Simple and consistent behaviour across all data types and better performance. `NaN` exists and is a float. It is **not** missing data, but the result of mathematical operations that don't return numbers. [Here](https://www.dontusethiscode.com/blog/2025-01-22_null_vs_nan_applied.html) is a blog that goes into this in details. ::: :::: ## Group by [Here](https://labs.quansight.org/blog/dataframe-group-by) is a great blog post by Marco Gorelli comparing the ease of use of passing expressions to groups in Polars compared to pandas. I am using his code here and doing some timing on it to look at efficiency. ### Create DataFrames Import libraries: ::: {#7043b9a0 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import polars as pl ``` ::: ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="49%"} The pandas DataFrame: ::: {#59959d3e .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.DataFrame( { "id": [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2], "sales": [4, 1, 2, 7, 6, 7], "views": [3, 1, 2, 8, 6, 7] } ) ``` ::: ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="49%"} The Polars equivalent: ::: {#b2c1d5aa .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.DataFrame( { "id": [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2], "sales": [4, 1, 2, 7, 6, 7], "views": [3, 1, 2, 8, 6, 7] } ) ``` ::: ::: :::: ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="49%"} The pandas DataFrame: ::: {#f6440604 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(df_pd) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` id sales views 0 1 4 3 1 1 1 1 2 1 2 2 3 2 7 8 4 2 6 6 5 2 7 7 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="49%"} The Polars equivalent: ::: {#6384f4b3 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(df_pl) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` shape: (6, 3) ┌─────┬───────┬───────┐ │ id ┆ sales ┆ views │ │ --- ┆ --- ┆ --- │ │ i64 ┆ i64 ┆ i64 │ ╞═════╪═══════╪═══════╡ │ 1 ┆ 4 ┆ 3 │ │ 1 ┆ 1 ┆ 1 │ │ 1 ┆ 2 ┆ 2 │ │ 2 ┆ 7 ┆ 8 │ │ 2 ┆ 6 ┆ 6 │ │ 2 ┆ 7 ┆ 7 │ └─────┴───────┴───────┘ ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ### Marco Gorelli's example > Find the maximum value of `views`, where `sales` is greater than its mean, per `id`. ### Solutions in pandas The straightforward method most people will use: ::: {#bcae6e82 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.groupby('id').apply( lambda df_pd: df_pd[df_pd['sales'] > df_pd['sales'].mean()]['views'].max(), include_groups=False ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` id 1 3 2 8 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: Another option, but it requires 2 `groupby` expressions: ::: {#bfc4a407 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd[ df_pd['sales'] > df_pd.groupby('id')['sales'].transform('mean') ].groupby('id')['views'].max() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ``` id 1 3 2 8 Name: views, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: A solution people are unlikely to come up with: ::: {#34705fa6 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} gb = df_pd.groupby("id") mask = df_pd["sales"] > gb["sales"].transform("mean") df_pd["result"] = df_pd["views"].where(mask) gb["result"].max() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ``` id 1 3.0 2 8.0 Name: result, dtype: float64 ``` ::: ::: ### The solution in Polars ```{.python} df_pl.group_by('id').agg( pl.col('views').filter(pl.col('sales') > pl.mean('sales')).max() ) ``` ::: {#f1c8dd10 .cell execution_count=10} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` shape: (2, 2) ┌─────┬───────┐ │ id ┆ views │ │ --- ┆ --- │ │ i64 ┆ i64 │ ╞═════╪═══════╡ │ 2 ┆ 8 │ │ 1 ┆ 3 │ └─────┴───────┘ ``` ::: ::: Much simpler ... ### Timing of pandas solutions ```{.python} %%timeit df_pd.groupby('id').apply( lambda df_pd: df_pd[df_pd['sales'] > df_pd['sales'].mean()]['views'].max(), include_groups=False ) ``` ``` 1.04 ms ± 1.33 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` The solution most people are likely to use is by far the slowest. ```{.python} %%timeit df_pd[ df_pd['sales'] > df_pd.groupby('id')['sales'].transform('mean') ].groupby('id')['views'].max() ``` ``` 678 μs ± 4.04 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` The best solution uses 2 `groupby` expressions. ```{.python} %%timeit gb = df_pd.groupby("id") mask = df_pd["sales"] > gb["sales"].transform("mean") df_pd["result"] = df_pd["views"].where(mask) gb["result"].max() ``` ``` 711 μs ± 952 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` This is almost as fast as the best solution. ### Timing of the Polars solution ```{.python} %%timeit df_pl.group_by('id').agg( pl.col('views').filter(pl.col('sales') > pl.mean('sales')).max() ) ``` ``` 110 μs ± 3.6 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10,000 loops each) ``` Polars is more straightforward and much faster. ### Conclusion Speedup of Polars compared to the best pandas method: **6**. \ Speedup compared to the method most people are likely to use: **9**. To pass expressions to groups, Polars has a more straightforward and efficient syntax. ## General performance comparisons :::{.note} The performance of tools can only be evaluated on optimized code: poorly written pandas code is a lot slower than efficient pandas code. To ensure fairness towards pandas, I use the best pandas code from [Alex Razoumov pandas course](https://wgpages.netlify.app/python2/python-13-pandas/) in which he benchmarks various syntaxes. ::: ### Example 1: FizzBuzz [FizzBuzz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fizz_buzz#Programming) is a programming exercise based on a children game which consists of counting to `n`, replacing: - any number divisible by `3` with `Fizz`, - any number divisible by `5` with `Buzz`, - any number divisible by both `3` and `5` with `FizzBuzz`. In his pandas course, Alex compares multiple methods and shows that [the best method uses masks](https://wgpages.netlify.app/python2/python-13-pandas/#three-solutions-to-a-classification-problem). Let's see how Polars fares in comparison to pandas' best method. First, let's load the packages we need and set the length of the series: ::: {#44f0282a .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import polars as pl n = 10_000 ``` ::: And let's make sure that the code works. With pandas: ::: {#ac8d4bad .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" print(df_pd) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Count FizzBuzz 0 1 1 1 2 2 2 3 Fizz 3 4 4 4 5 Buzz ... ... ... 9995 9996 Fizz 9996 9997 9997 9997 9998 9998 9998 9999 Fizz 9999 10000 Buzz [10000 rows x 2 columns] ``` ::: ::: With Polars: ::: {#b928f314 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl = df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) print(df_pl) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` shape: (10_000, 2) ┌───────┬──────────┐ │ Count ┆ FizzBuzz │ │ --- ┆ --- │ │ i64 ┆ str │ ╞═══════╪══════════╡ │ 1 ┆ 1 │ │ 2 ┆ 2 │ │ 3 ┆ Fizz │ │ 4 ┆ 4 │ │ 5 ┆ Buzz │ │ … ┆ … │ │ 9996 ┆ Fizz │ │ 9997 ┆ 9997 │ │ 9998 ┆ 9998 │ │ 9999 ┆ Fizz │ │ 10000 ┆ Buzz │ └───────┴──────────┘ ``` ::: ::: But it gets much better with [lazy evaluation](polars_lazy.qmd). First, we create a LazyFrame instead of a DataFrame. The query is not evaluated but a graph is created. This allows the query optimizer to combine operations and perform [optimizations](https://docs.pola.rs/user-guide/lazy/optimizations/) where possible, very much the way compilers work. To evaluate the query and get a result, we use the `collect` method. Let's make sure that the lazy Polars code gives us the same result: With the Polars lazy API: ::: {#e7bc8320 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl_lazy_collected = df_pl_lazy.collect() print(df_pl_lazy_collected) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` shape: (10_000, 2) ┌───────┬──────────┐ │ Count ┆ FizzBuzz │ │ --- ┆ --- │ │ i64 ┆ str │ ╞═══════╪══════════╡ │ 1 ┆ 1 │ │ 2 ┆ 2 │ │ 3 ┆ Fizz │ │ 4 ┆ 4 │ │ 5 ┆ Buzz │ │ … ┆ … │ │ 9996 ┆ Fizz │ │ 9997 ┆ 9997 │ │ 9998 ┆ 9998 │ │ 9999 ┆ Fizz │ │ 10000 ┆ Buzz │ └───────┴──────────┘ ``` ::: ::: #### Timing pandas: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" ``` ``` 3.4 ms ± 65.4 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each) ``` Polars: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) ``` ``` 648 μs ± 2.21 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` Polars lazy API: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl_lazy_collected = df_pl_lazy.collect() ``` ``` 460 μs ± 1.79 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` That's a speedup of **5** for the Eager API and a speedup of **7** for the lazy API. These speedups highly depend on the types and sizes of the problems. How much faster Polars is does vary, what doesn’t vary is that it is always much faster. #### Memory usage ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="32%"} **pandas** ::: {#251dec6c .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ``` Index 132 Count 80000 FizzBuzz 80000 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 80,000 * 2 = \ 160,132 bytes \ **≈ 160 KB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars** ::: {#8e648949 .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=15} ``` 119409 ``` ::: ::: 119,409 bytes \ **≈ 119 KB** 160,132 / 119,409 ≈ 1.3 \ Footprint 1.3 times smaller. ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars lazy API** Same as Polars eager: \ if you collect the entire DataFrame as we did here, the lazy API does not reduce the memory footprint. ::: :::: ### Example 2: medium dataset Here I am using [a jeopardy dataset](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv) that Alex uses in his pandas course. Shape: 216,930 rows, 7 columns. The goal is to subset this DataFrame for the history category and get the shape of the resulting DataFrame. First, let's make sure that the code works. pandas: ::: {#a289669e .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"].shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=16} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: Polars: ::: {#da0f49f3 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=17} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: Polars lazy API: ::: {#f9f212fa .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pl_lazy_subset_collected = df_pl_lazy.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").collect() df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=18} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: :::{.note} To create a LazyFrame from a file you use one of the `scan_*` methods instead of the `read_*` methods. ::: #### Timing pandas: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pd = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"].shape ``` ``` 1.17 s ± 5.2 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` Polars: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pl = pl.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").shape ``` ``` 778 ms ± 30.5 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` That's a speedup of **1.5**. Polars lazy API timing: ```{.python} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv") df_pl_lazy.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").collect().shape ``` ``` 67.7 ms ± 3.87 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each) ``` With the lazy API, the speedup ramps up to **17**. #### Memory usage ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="30%"} ::: {#eff76b42 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=19} ``` Index 132 Show Number 1735440 Air Date 1735440 Round 1735440 Category 1735440 Value 1735440 Question 1735440 Answer 1735440 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 1,735,440 * 7 = \ 12,148,212 bytes \ **≈ 12 MB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#0bdf8d14 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=20} ``` 31606250 ``` ::: ::: 31,606,250 bytes \ **≈ 31 MB** 31,606,250 / 12,148,212 ≈ 2.6 \ Footprint 2.6 times *bigger*. ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#0da1381c .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=21} ``` 48774 ``` ::: ::: 48,774 bytes \ **≈ 49 KB** 12,148,212 / 48,774 ≈ 249 \ Footprint **249 times smaller**. If you only collect a subset of the data, the memory footprint drops dramatically. ::: :::: ## Data too big to fit in memory ### Example of large dataset Let's play with data from the [GBIF](https://www.gbif.org/) website for free and open access to biodiversity data. The [Southern African Bird Atlas Project 2](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/906e6978-e292-4a8b-9c39-adf6bb0f3323) [@https://doi.org/10.15468/dl.gqwnm2] contains a raw CSV file of 42 GB and an interpreted CSV file of 12.3 GB. Let's look at the latter: Shape: 25,687,526 rows, 50 columns. From that dataset, I want a list of species from the genus *Passer* (Old World sparrows). ### The importance of file format Text-based formats (CSV, JSON) are not suitable for large files. [Apache Parquet](https://parquet.apache.org/) is a binary, machine-optimized, column-oriented file format with efficient encoding and compression and it is considered the industry-standard file format for large tabular data.
| ||| File type ||| Size | | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | | File downloaded from GBIF ||| Zipped CSV ||| 2.2 GB | | Uncompressed file ||| CSV ||| 12.3 GB | | Ideal file format ||| [Parquet]{.emph} ||| [0.5 GB]{.emph} |
In addition to being **15 times (!!) smaller**, the Parquet file is **a lot faster to read & write**. ### Format conversion Here is a way to convert this file if it fits in memory: ```{.python} # Read in the CSV file in a Polars DataFrame df = pl.read_csv("sa_birds.csv", separator="\t") # Export the DataFrame in a Parquet file df.write_parquet("sa_birds.parquet") ``` :::{.note} If the file doesn't fit in memory, you can use Dask (which also uses Apache Arrow) on a distributed system. ::: ### Native Apache Arrow support With its native support for the columnar Apache Arrow format, Polars is ideal to work with Parquet files. pandas can work with Parquet files but this can lead to issues due to the inconsistent way it handles missing data (e.g. see [this SO question](https://stackoverflow.com/q/54866522/9210961), [this issue](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/55721), [this post](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-au/answers/questions/5534487/timestamp-column-from-parquet-file-(created-with-p#:~:text=Venkat%20Reddy%20Navari,you%20have%20any%20further%20queries.))). ```{.python} %%timeit # pandas sa_birds_pd = pd.read_parquet( "sa_birds.parquet" ) ``` ``` 1min 10s ± 7.76 s per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` ```{.python} %%timeit # Polars sa_birds_pl = pl.read_parquet( "sa_birds.parquet" ) ``` ``` 2.47 s ± 756 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` Speedup: 70 / 2.47 ≈ **28**. [Reading Parquet files in pandas is also A LOT slower!]{.emph} ### RAM required ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="49%"} **pandas** ```{.python} sa_birds_pd.memory_usage() ``` ``` Index 132 gbifID 205500208 datasetKey 205500208 occurrenceID 205500208 kingdom 205500208 phylum 205500208 class 205500208 order 205500208 ... dtype: int64 ``` 132 + 205,500,208 * 50 = \ 10,275,010,532 bytes \ **≈ 10 GB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="49%"} **Polars** ```{.python} sa_birds_pl.estimated_size() ``` ``` 12291023880 ``` 12,291,023,880 bytes \ **≈ 12 GB** ::: :::: Let's image that you have only 16 GB of RAM on your machine. Running a browser and a few small applications take about 6 GB out of that. This dataset is already too big to process on your laptop. If you need to play with the raw data or a larger dataset such as the [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) of 1,775,781,186 rows and 50 columns (**CSV file of 3 TB for the raw data**), you will need A LOT of memory. ### What are your options? In pandas or Polars: - you can select only the columns you are interested, - you can read and process the file in chunks and try to combine the results. [If you only need to run queries on the data however, the Polars lazy API is the answer.]{.emph} ### Create a LazyFrame You can create a LazyFrame (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly): ::: {#44793c60 .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_parquet("sa_birds.parquet") ``` ::: Run a query on it (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly): ::: {#f0b340f4 .cell execution_count=24} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_df_lazy = df_pl_lazy.filter( pl.col("genus") == "Passer" ).select(pl.col("species")).unique() ``` ::: ### Collect result Now, you collect the result (this uses memory and takes time to compute) and turn the DataFrame into a Python list: ::: {#250413b7 .cell execution_count=25} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_ls = passer_df_lazy.collect().get_column("species").to_list() ``` ::: Depending on the subset returned by your query, the memory usage will vary. Here, it is minuscule (79 bytes): ::: {#6ba3b93a .cell execution_count=26} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_df_lazy.collect().estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=25} ``` 79 ``` ::: ::: ### Et voilà ::: {#6456efa4 .cell execution_count=27} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(passer_ls) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ['Passer diffusus', 'Passer motitensis', 'Passer griseus', 'Passer domesticus', 'Passer melanurus'] ``` ::: ::: With this method, as long as the data itself fits on a drive and the queries don't return huge subsets of data, you can run queries on giant datasets such as the 3 TB [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) with a small amount of RAM. Note that the queries can contain complex expressions. What matters is the size of the subset you need to collect at the end. ## Pandas v2 Pandas is trying to fight back: v 2.0 came with optional Arrow support instead of NumPy, then [it became the default engine](https://dataalgo.medium.com/pandas-2-0-ditches-numpy-for-pyarrow-what-you-need-to-know-cbba4cb60249), but performance remains way below that of Polars (e.g. in [DataCamp benchmarks](https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/high-performance-data-manipulation-in-python-pandas2-vs-polars), [official benchmarks](https://pola.rs/posts/benchmarks/), many blog posts for [whole scripts](https://medium.com/@asimandia/benchmarking-performance-polars-vs-vaex-vs-pandas-f1c889dccc12) or [individual tasks](https://medium.com/cuenex/pandas-2-0-vs-polars-the-ultimate-battle-a378eb75d6d1)). And the problems with missing data and syntax remains. Not to mention the lack of lazy API. ## Comparison with other frameworks :::{.info} Comparisons between Polars and distributed (Dask, Ray, Spark) or GPU (RAPIDS) libraries aren't the most pertinent since they can be used in *combination with* Polars and the benefits can thus be combined. It only makes sense to compare Polars with other libraries occupying the same "niche" such as pandas or Vaex. For [Vaex](https://github.com/vaexio/vaex), [some benchmark found it twice slower](https://medium.com/@asimandia/benchmarking-performance-polars-vs-vaex-vs-pandas-f1c889dccc12), but this could have changed with recent developments. One framework performing better than Polars in some benchmarks is [datatable](https://github.com/h2oai/datatable) (derived from the R package [data.table](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/data.table/index.html)), but it hasn't been developed for a year—a sharp contrast with the fast development of Polars. ::: ## Migrating from Pandas Read [the migration guide](https://docs.pola.rs/user-guide/migration/pandas/#selecting-data): it will help you write Polars code rather than "literally translated" Pandas code that runs, but doesn't make use of Polars' strengths. The differences in style mostly come from the fact that Polars runs in parallel. ## Conclusion Polars: - handles missing data in a consistent and sensible way, - has a clear syntax, - allows passing expressions to group by in a convenient way, - is very fast, - uses multithreading automatically, - is extremely memory efficient with the lazy API, - uses the industry standard Apache Arrow columnar format, - works perfectly with Parquet files, - works out-of-core with the lazy API. **Start using Polars now!** **And use the lazy API.** ![](img/lazy_polar_bear.jpg){fig-align="center"}
